danger_signals.visual_form: enemy sprites moving down screen in organized patterns
danger_signals.meaning: incoming threat requiring evasion or engagement

danger_signals.visual_form: large enemy sprite filling significant screen area
danger_signals.meaning: major threat; high-priority target with substantial firepower

danger_signals.visual_form: bullet sprites traveling toward player ship
play field limits marked by screen borders
danger_signals.meaning: spatial constraint; player ship cannot escape screen perimeter

safety_signals.visual_form: black/dark background with minimal sprite activity
safety_signals.meaning: brief respite; safe zone for repositioning

safety_signals.visual_form: distinctive colored/animated sprite dropping from defeated enemies
safety_signals.meaning: beneficial item; weapon upgrade or scoring bonus available for collection

safety_signals.visual_form: absence of enemy sprites in previously occupied screen area
safety_signals.meaning: threat eliminated; temporary safety until next wave

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: player ship positioned at bottom center of screen; distinct from enemy sprites

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: alien/mechanical enemy sprites; distinct silhouette from player ship
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: multiple enemy types with varied sprite designs (small fighters, larger cruisers, boss entities)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: enemies spawn from top of screen; move in coordinated downward patterns; occupy upper/middle screen space

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: high sprite density; projectiles visible on screen; animated explosion effects
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies fire at player; player ship responsive to input; constant movement of all entities

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: reduced sprite count; clear screen areas; slower visual activity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy formations retreat or clear; brief pause in projectile fire; player repositioning opportunity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: single large sprite dominating screen area; intensive projectile patterns; more elaborate animation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: boss sprite moves autonomously in scripted patterns; fires concentrated projectile streams; requires sustained engagement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: defeated enemy formations within proximity glow or flash; bonus text/indicators appear on screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player earns enhanced points for sequential kills; visual feedback confirms multiplier status

environmental_storytelling.example: continuous downward screen scroll suggests forward movement through space; establishes sense of journey through enemy-occupied territory
different enemy types and boss designs suggest escalating military opposition; visual complexity increases with progression
hidden destructible environment elements suggest hostile territory with embedded obstacles and bonuses; encourages thorough spatial exploration
dropped items from defeated enemies communicate available upgrades; visual distinction of power-up types indicates weapon variety

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: responds to input; moves horizontally; can fire continuously; changes position based on player command
descends screen in formation; fires projectiles at player; follows predictable movement patterns
occupies large sprite space; executes complex movement patterns; fires intensive projectile patterns; persists across extended engagement
drops from defeated enemies; descends screen slowly; collected on contact with player ship; grants temporary or permanent benefits

notes: Star Soldier (1988 NES) operates as a pure arcade-style scrolling shooter with minimal narrative world-state. The game communicates danger, threat escalation, and player capability through sprite-based visual language: enemy density, projectile patterns, and boss design complexity create progression. Safety is indicated through cleared screen areas and power-up appearances. No faction identity system exists beyond player vs. enemy binary opposition. World-state transitions are purely mechanical (wave patterns, difficulty escalation) rather than narrative. Environmental storytelling relies on enemy variety and spatial progression. The 'Caravan Mode' time-attack mechanic introduces secondary world-state (score multiplier zones) that manifests through proximity-based visual feedback. No political or social faction systems present.
